#49 Exploring Ono Castle Ruins, Fukuoka, Japan

June 29, 2024 1 hour 45 minutes

All transcripts are machine generated.

Transcript

They just have an unreality to them. Sort of similar, just the way this haze has settled over everything. So just at a curiosity, I went a little ways, but it's just so very obvious when you're not going the right way. Because even though that particular part was very light, it wasn't trodden at all. There was no kind of footpath. There was a really old sign that was just like nothing on it anymore, just like the skeleton of a sign that obviously hadn't been anything for a long fucking time. Whereas the other way, despite going up, what signposted had steps, had pink ribbons on the trees, like it's just blazingly clear. So this is the way, go this way, so I went back up, and it basically just looped me around back to where I started. So I walked up around a peak, it was a nice walk, and again, because I gave myself a lot of free time today, it's like, well, cool, alright, that was a little thing. But yeah, now at this point, I think I just can't trust that there are any more paths through the woods to get me off this mountain.

There might be, but I sure don't see them, so just walking down the road, not the best, you know, again, it's a very quiet road.

So it's just a little two-lane road, there's definitely not quite enough room for me to be here, there's no walking area.

But there's not that many cars, they all see me well before they get to me. Not the best, but I just got to get out of here at this point, you know. No more lolligagging, no more walking around in circles, time to just get the fuck out of here. Speaking of, let me just check to make sure my blue dot is still going.

Boop! Yeah, I mean, I'm walking downhill, I don't know how I could possibly be going the wrong way, but just in case. And nope, I'm going the right way, heading down toward a joyful, which I think is a family restaurant, and home Plaza Nafco, and a gas station.

Wow, it still looks like a ways, but straight on till morning. I don't know how else to get off of this mountain.

So yeah, what I wanted to say about the Buddhist statues, because yeah, that fork in the road, I felt it very distinctly. Like, I mean, this is going to be offensive, but this is really how I see it. So I mean, if you're religious, I don't know, take this as an opportunity to try to peer into the mind of the atheist. It feels like the biggest game of telephone in the world, or purple monkey dishwasher, you might call it. You know, the game where you whisper a phrase into someone's ear, and they whisper it into the next person's ear, and the next person's ear. And then you see, after eight or nine people, what comes out the other side? Like, is it possible to spread the message through that many people without it getting corrupted? And you know, it's like funny. It's like, the thing that comes out the other side is purple monkey dishwasher. It's some weird thing. That's just kind of how my brain thinks of religion, is it's like, and this is what I presume about religious people. I could be wrong, but this is how I see you, in case you're curious, is like, if there's these stories that stretch back thousands and thousands of years, and millions and millions and billions of people have passed along this story, and they all believe it, then surely it can't be a lie, right? Surely that many people couldn't believe it, and it couldn't endure this long if it wasn't true. That's how I presume religious people think of things.

Where my brain just isn't wired that way. The way I think of it, I mean, maybe it's just like a dangerous level of narcissism or something, I don't know.

That girl Anna from Columbia, when I was talking to her, she grew up Catholic as well. And we were saying how interesting it was because she's in her 20s. I'm in my 40s that in both our cases, we had, you know, grandparents that were real into this type of stuff. I mean, my mom has 12 brothers and sisters. If that's not fucking Catholic, I don't know what is. My grandmother's name was Molly for God's sake. It's the most Catholic family that could ever be. And then my mom, it's always a little hard to pinpoint. At this point, I don't think she's very religious, but she certainly made us go to church as 1:20:00 kids. And I don't know how much of that was just because, you know, that's what she had to do. So just keep it going. All of her friends were through church. So I mean, I think it could even just be that. It might be as simple as it was awkward if her kids didn't go to church so she made us go to church. But yeah, like I knew all of the stand up here, kneel here, say this when, you know, I knew all the rituals of Catholic church. They just pretended to make their parents happy. I was like, I'm sorry, I'm not fucking doing that. Are you fucking kidding? I was like into religion a bit when I was younger. I remember up till like third or fourth grade, there were these like Jesus comic books and stuff that I would read. And I'd be able to pair it back all the parables and crap. So I think this is interesting because Japan is so like they take such a light touch with religion. They're not really known as a strongly religious country. And one of the little anecdotes I heard is that something like 80% of people consider themselves shinto. But about 80% of people also consider themselves Buddhist. That's enough with the Buddhist temples. There's so many Buddhist temples. I would like to go on the walk for the sake of the walk. I'd like to go on the adventure for the sake of the adventure.
